target.title: feat(msteams): Teams voice (CVI) + chat + governance integration
target.body:
> **Stacked on #91438 — review the increment** (`b98d5a0a7a…fdb99003cb`, 51 commits): the foundation is #91438. The full diff includes #91438's commits only until that PR lands; this branch then rebases to exactly the increment. A commit→feature map is below.

## Summary

Builds on the `msteams` voice provider (#91438) along six lines:

- **Correctness / security** — fixes the realtime **self-answer (echo) bug**, makes the bridge handshake **replay-proof**, enforces group etiquette and **verbal interrupts deterministically** (in code at the audio egress, not model-trusted), and fixes outbound call-backs that **spoke to a still-ringing phone** (and the context-bleed that caused).
- **Call UX** — verbal interrupts ("stop" / wake-phrase forms, **English and Arabic**), notify call-backs (deliver → hang up, un-clipped), **greet-by-name** from the roster, **bilingual Arabic/English**, **DTMF / IVR** ("press 1 to…").
- **CVI** — retroactive vision ("what did the earlier slide say?"), scene-change + camera/screen ambient vision, captioned/slideshow visual sharing as a **picture-in-picture overlay** over the avatar, a **"thinking"** expression, per-thread sessions.
- **Meeting productivity** — opt-in **end-of-meeting minutes** posted to the Teams chat/thread, on-demand **"summarize the meeting"** mid-call, a **Word-openable minutes document**, and **per-person attribution** from unmixed meeting audio.
- **Chat** — a Teams **"Ask OpenClaw about this" message action** routes a selected chat message to the agent; opt-in **voice-message transcription** folds spoken clips into the agent's turn.
- **Governance** — opt-in **audit-log channel** mirroring agent replies and **DLP-aware outbound redaction**.

A post-feature **self-review hardening pass** then audited the whole increment and fixed everything it found (2 critical, 13 bugs, 1 security finding, structural refactors) — see "Review hardening" below.

| Layer | Themes |
| --- | --- |
| **Correctness / security** | echo self-answer fix (`suppressInputDuringPlayback` → on), replay-proof HMAC tuples, deterministic group gate on realtime, playout clock for echo + notify drain, **greet-on-answer** (outbound call-backs no longer deliver to a ringing phone) |
| **Call UX** | verbal interrupts (EN + AR), notify call-backs (deliver → hang up), **roster greeting by name**, **bilingual Arabic/English**, **DTMF / IVR** |
| **CVI** | retroactive vision (16-keyframe ring), scene-change + camera/screen vision, captions + slideshow, **PiP overlay**, "thinking" expression, per-thread sessions |
| **Meeting productivity** | end-of-meeting recap to chat/thread, on-demand `/summarize`, DOCX minutes artifact, per-speaker attribution (unmixed audio) |
| **Chat** | "Ask OpenClaw about this" message action, voice-message transcription |
| **Governance** | audit-log channel, DLP outbound redaction |
| **Review hardening** | single per-call cleanup owner, deliver-to-ringing (streaming), un-hangup-able call, 10 more bugs, `withConsultGuards` + structural refactors |

## ⚠️ Behavior changes

- `realtime.suppressInputDuringPlayback` default **flipped off → on**: default-off *was* the live self-answer bug — the bot answered its own echo on every realtime deployment. Set `false` to restore; tune via `realtime.echoSuppressionWindowMs` / `realtime.echoBargeInRms`.
- Outbound call-backs now honor `outbound.defaultMode` (default **notify** = state the result, then hang up after the audio drains) **and speak only once the callee answers** (recording-active), not when the bridge connects — on **both** the realtime and streaming paths. Previously they greeted on connect — i.e. while the phone was still ringing — so the result was lost and the idle model could surface unrelated context. Set `"conversation"` to keep the lingering behavior.
- `msteams.meetingRecap` (default **off**) — when on, the bot posts meeting minutes to the caller's Teams chat (1:1) or the meeting thread (group) at call end. The bot posting unprompted is gated behind this opt-in.
- `msteams.bilingual` (default **off**) — when on, the realtime model is pinned to detect/mirror Arabic↔English and translate on request. The deterministic layers (verbal interrupts, visemes) understand Arabic regardless.
- `channels.msteams.transcribeVoiceMessages` (default **off**) — when on, inbound Teams voice clips are transcribed (STT cost) and folded into the agent's message text.
- `channels.msteams.auditChannel` (unset by default) — when set to a conversation id, agent replies are mirrored there for compliance review.
- `channels.msteams.dlp.enabled` (default **off**) — when on, built-in categories and `customPatterns` are redacted from **every** outbound surface (block messages, streamed replies, edits, adaptive cards). Because per-token streaming cannot be redacted retroactively (a secret completing across chunk boundaries would already be on screen), DLP also downgrades `streaming.mode: "partial"` to `"progress"`: the live card shows working status and the redacted full reply lands on it in place.

## 🛡️ Security & correctness hardening

- **Single-use HMAC upgrade tuples (replay guard)** — a captured handshake replayed within the HMAC window could open a ghost session after the live call ended. `(callId, timestamp, signature)` tuples are single-use; a legitimate reconnect carries a fresh timestamp; only verified tuples are recorded (no unauthenticated map growth) and entries expire with the **timestamp's own validity window** (a future-dated handshake from worker clock skew can't outlive its record). Tested: connect OK → exact replay 401 → fresh-timestamp reconnect OK.
- **Deterministic group-call gate (realtime)** — every finished caller turn is evaluated with the same `shouldRespondToGroupTurn` core the streaming path uses; an unaddressed meeting turn's reply **audio is dropped at the egress**. 1:1 never gates.
- **Echo guard on a playout clock** — the realtime model streams faster than realtime, so last-send time is not play-time; the guard and the notify drain both key on a playout estimate (accumulated chunk durations, collapsed on barge-in). Regression test: burst 10 s of audio → echo still suppressed at +2 s. **Opening-greeting hardening (live-reported):** until the caller's first real turn the guard allows no barge-in, so the bot's own greeting echoing back on a speakerphone can't make the model interrupt itself and re-greet in a loop while the caller is silent.
- **Greet-on-answer (outbound call-backs)** — the greeting (the delivered result) now fires on recording-active (the callee answered), not on bridge-connect; this both delivers the result at the right time and closes the idle window where the model would otherwise free-associate from its context.

## 📞 Call experience

- **Deterministic verbal interrupts** — "stop" / "wait" / "hold on" / "never mind" handled in code: streaming cuts playback **and suppresses the reply**; realtime flushes the worker queue. Whole-utterance match with filler **and configured wake-phrase stripping** — *"⟨name⟩, stop"* cuts as instantly as bare *"stop"*; *"stop by the store"* never triggers. **Unicode-aware**: Arabic interrupts ("توقف", "خلاص", …) cut identically, with tashkeel-safe normalization (same `\p{L}` approach as the group gate).
- **Roster greeting** — the model greets the caller by first name (from the Teams roster) and addresses participants by the per-utterance speaker label.
- **Bilingual Arabic/English** (opt-in) — detect, mirror, switch-with-caller, translate on request; the viseme estimator also maps Arabic graphemes so lip-sync isn't RMS-only in Arabic.
- **DTMF / IVR** — in-band keypad tones from the caller are surfaced to the realtime model as a user message ("the caller pressed 1"), so the agent can run "press 1 to…" flows. *(Worker companion forwards the tone.)*
- **Notify call-backs deliver-and-hang-up without clipping** — drain-aware (playout clock) + `notifyHangupDelaySec` grace; a failed hangup falls back to a direct teardown.

## 🎥 CVI extensions

- **Retroactive vision (frame history)** — a ring of 16 scene-change keyframes per call; `look_at_screen scope:"history"` attaches up to 6 timestamped, attributed keyframes to **one budgeted consult**. No eager OCR-per-keyframe.
- **Scene-change-driven ambient vision** — frames reach the realtime model on arrival; **camera + screen simultaneously**, per-source dedup + labels.
- **`show_to_caller` captions + slideshow + PiP overlay** — caption strip on the tile; multi-image results play as a paced slideshow; images ride as a **bottom-right inset over the live avatar** (`display.image mode:"overlay"`) so the bot stays visible. Additive protocol; older renderers fall back to fullscreen.
- **"Thinking" avatar expression** while a tool runs; the reply re-cues the real emotion.
- **Real lip-sync timing** — the elevenlabs provider's telephony synthesis uses `/with-timestamps` and surfaces per-character alignment (optional field through `textToSpeechTelephony`); msteams `speech.marks` now carry each sound's *actual* start time instead of an even spread, falling back to the estimator when alignment is absent. Audio never depends on timing: a failing `/with-timestamps` endpoint degrades to plain synthesis.
- **Per-thread sessions** — `sessionScope: "per-thread"` keys the agent session by the Teams meeting/chat thread; parallel conversations stay isolated. PSTN falls through to per-phone.

## 📝 Meeting productivity

- **End-of-meeting recap** (opt-in `msteams.meetingRecap`) — key points / decisions / action items posted to the caller's Teams chat (1:1) or **into the meeting thread** (group) when the call ends.
- **On-demand minutes** — "summarize the meeting" mid-call posts the running minutes to chat without ending the call (`post_meeting_minutes` tool).
- **Word-openable minutes artifact** — the recap attaches a formatted document the caller can open in Word.
- **Per-person attribution** — with unmixed meeting audio the worker tags each turn with the speaker, so minutes and transcripts read "Sara: …" instead of one mixed stream.

## 💬 Chat & governance

- **"Ask OpenClaw about this" message action** — a Teams message-context command (`composeExtension/submitAction`) quotes the selected chat message into a prompt and dispatches it through the normal message path, so the answer posts back into the conversation. *(Requires the Teams app manifest to declare the command: `composeExtensions > commands`, `type: "action"`, `context: ["message"]`, `id: "askOpenClaw"`.)*
- **Voice messages** (opt-in `transcribeVoiceMessages`) — inbound Teams audio clips are transcribed and folded into the agent's turn as a quoted block; a clip that fails transcription keeps its attachment placeholder.
- **Audit-log channel** (opt-in `auditChannel`) — agent replies are mirrored to a named Teams conversation for compliance review. The mirror fires at the **delivery choke point**, so natively-streamed replies (the DM default) are in the trail too, not only block deliveries; the loop guard keeps the audit channel's own traffic out.
- **DLP outbound redaction** (opt-in `dlp.enabled`) — built-in categories plus custom patterns are redacted with a configurable placeholder, on **every** outbound surface: block messages, streamed replies (see Behavior changes), message **edits**, and **adaptive cards** (deep-redacted per string value). The audit excerpt is redacted **before** truncation so the cap can never slice a secret into an unrecognizable fragment.

## 🔎 Review hardening (post-feature self-audit)

After the feature work, a full self-review of the increment found and fixed — each with a regression test:

- **2 critical** — realtime mode never wired the CallManager (the stale-call reaper force-ended connected outbound callbacks ~2 min in); the msteams safe-allowlist inbound default was dead code (msteams+streaming rejected ALL inbound calls in production config). (`4b38320fa4`)
- **1 security** — HMAC replay records now expire at `ts+window`, not `now+window`, closing a replay window for future-dated (clock-skewed) handshakes. (`428f458b73`)
- **4 state-leak paths, one design fix** — a single `releaseCallState` owner that every teardown funnels through: host-initiated closes no longer suppress `onSessionEnd` (dead calls reported in-progress forever); a realtime provider WS drop no longer strands the caller on an **un-hangup-able call**; manager hangups release vision/recording state (~1–2 MB/call); a declined outbound call no longer leaves its CallRecord active against `maxConcurrentCalls`. (`a112973df8`)
- **9 individual bugs** — deliver-to-ringing on the *streaming* path; custom-tool calls stalling the model turn; unbounded/misattributed transcript coalescing; the message action being mention-gated to silence in group chats; history-look poisoning the live vision cache; the minutes tool skipping the recording gate + the task tool acking a no-op; empty-`aadId` cross-caller session bleed; lost-and-budget-burning ambient vision pushes; voice-clip placeholder mishandling. (`495266a243`)
- **Structural refactors** so this class of bug has fewer places to hide — `withConsultGuards` (one copy of the recording-gate/deps/error/filler boilerplate for all agent-running tools), `emitCallEnded`, a single hoisted consult session key, the tool surface split into `msteams-realtime-tools.ts`, and the msteams runtime no longer building the webhook-plane realtime handler Teams never uses. (`e4b6a523fa`)
- **Bilingual honored by the deterministic layers** — verbal interrupts match Arabic (Unicode normalization + Arabic phrase set) and the viseme map covers Arabic graphemes. (`0867a7c3d7`)
- **4 governance bypasses (the review's most severe findings)** — DLP/audit only covered the block-delivery text path. Now: native streaming redacted (token streaming downgrades to progress under DLP — append-only streams can't retract a secret that completes across chunks); the audit mirror moved to the delivery choke point so streamed replies are in the trail; message edits and adaptive cards redact (closing the "edit the secret in" / "put it in a card" loopholes); the secret pattern catches segmented `sk-proj-`/`sk-ant-` keys and the audit excerpt redacts before truncating. (`cc017b6c21`)
- **Poll-card DLP redaction (Codex review follow-up)** — the ClawSweeper Codex pass caught a remaining card surface: `sendPollMSTeams` sent the poll Adaptive Card unredacted (the S3 fix only covered the generic adaptive-card path), so a secret in a poll question or option label reached the wire. The poll's agent-supplied question and option strings are now redacted before the card is built — visible text only, leaving the internal option indices and `pollId` intact so vote matching is unaffected. (`83e4bbb1da`)

## Commit → feature map (increment beyond #91438)

| Feature | Commits |
| --- | --- |
| Refactors (vision store, tts playback) | `b98d5a0a7a`, `163a2ea3da` |
| Echo / self-answer fix (+ knobs, playout clock) | `ef59b2d1a9`, `6db869ea08`, `29dbaef7f7` |
| Captions + slideshow | `c474f6861b`, `2269079f5c` |
| Notify call-backs (mode + drain + greet-on-answer) | `d25305f408`, `c19100ad14`, `660e398013` |
| Scene-change + camera/screen vision | `cd60d3afd8`, `7c1da2d81e` |
| "Thinking" expression | `249aa94f50` |
| Replay guard (+ ts-window expiry) | `ae3da5da40`, `428f458b73` |
| Deterministic group gate | `53ee788114` |
| Retroactive vision (keyframe ring) | `252b1b5b83` |
| Per-thread sessions | `c1e09b8c9e` |
| Verbal interrupts (+ wake-phrase form, + Arabic) | `16a643e17c`, `83c527acb7`, `0867a7c3d7` |
| Meeting recap + thread + on-demand + DOCX + attribution | `0281b0b03c`, `8087a39a78`, `1d7b30d133`, `46aa7627e1`, `e3237338d0`, `7f5dc7befb` |
| PiP overlay (#17) | `7cc0812a8e` |
| Roster greeting (#20) | `a71b85f1f5` |
| Bilingual (#19) | `e223fc69f9`, `0867a7c3d7` |
| DTMF / IVR (#21) | `48ab5ce268` |
| "Ask OpenClaw about this" message action (#10) | `8ed41203b7` |
| Voice-message transcription (#13) | `72584f33fd` |
| DLP outbound redaction (#16) | `8fbfbb1118` |
| Audit-log channel (#15) | `47ee2c0b18`, `edc096abca` |
| Review hardening (critical / security / bugs / refactors) | `4b38320fa4`, `2fd4aeab49`, `a112973df8`, `495266a243`, `e4b6a523fa` |
| Governance bypass closure (streaming/edit/card DLP + audit choke point) | `cc017b6c21` |
| Regenerated bundled channel metadata (governance keys were unconfigurable) | `f8fd757c60` |
| Real viseme timing (ElevenLabs with-timestamps alignment) | `a06bc05c1f` |
| Poll-card DLP redaction (Codex review follow-up) | `83e4bbb1da` |
| Regenerated config artifacts after upstream rebase | `b4177e9faa` |
| Docs: Teams channel governance + voice/CVI config (#91438+#92081) | `1345426af8` |
| Fix: opening-greeting echo loop on speakerphone (msteams realtime) | `fdb99003cb` |

Each feature ships with unit tests (matcher/positive-negative units, display-path, history-ring, group-gate egress drop, replay 401/reconnect, recap prompt, DTMF mapping, message-action extraction, teardown/leak regressions, Arabic interrupt/viseme cases, etc.).

## ✅ Live validation (2026-06-12, real Teams calls)

> Foundation flows (base voice/video, group calls, background-task chat delivery, outbound callback) were separately live-proven on the #91438 foundation PR — see its Real-behavior-proof section; this PR’s diff includes those commits until #91438 lands.

Re-validated end-to-end on this branch's exact head against a production Teams tenant (gateway from source, companion media worker as a Windows service), in two calls:

- **Inbound 1:1 call (~7.5 min)** — roster greeting by name; no echo self-answer with the reply on open speakers; barge-in; deterministic verbal interrupts in **English, wake-phrase form, and Arabic ("توقف")**; bilingual Arabic↔English mirroring + on-request translation; `openclaw_agent_consult` with the **"thinking" avatar expression** held across six tool runs (worker log); emotion cues (Happy/Neutral transitions) tracking reply tone; **screen-share vision** (`look_at_screen`, scene-change awareness, retroactive keyframe history — two VBSS share windows in the worker log); **`show_to_caller`** rendered two images as a **PiP overlay with caption**, paced as a slideshow with exactly the coded hold times (4500 ms non-final / 5000 ms final).
- **Outbound notify call-back** (placed by an in-call background task): `outbound call placed → Established → recording-active gate opened → result spoken only after answer (greet-on-answer) → notify-mode auto-hangup after the audio drained` — complete, un-clipped delivery, clean teardown, no leaked call state.
- **Meeting productivity** — on-hangup recap fired (`posting meeting recap`) and the minutes landed in the Teams chat; in-call background task delivered its answer to chat.
- Both calls tore down cleanly (`connection closed` with no lingering state) — the review-hardening teardown paths behaving live.
- The validation also caught that the **governance keys were unconfigurable** (stale generated channel metadata + a Windows-silent regen script) — fixed in `f8fd757c60`; the gateway now starts with `dlp`/`transcribeVoiceMessages`/`auditChannel` configured.

**Chat & governance (same day, follow-up session):**

- **Audit mirror on streamed replies (S2)** — with `auditChannel` set to a dedicated group chat, every streamed DM reply produced a `🧾 Reply for <sender> in <conversation>: <excerpt>` line in the audit chat; the bot's own reply *inside* the audit chat produced no self-mirror (loop guard).
- **DLP on the wire (S1)** — a DM reply containing an email rendered as `…([REDACTED:email])` in the Teams client: the model emitted the address, the redaction layer scrubbed it on the **streamed** path — the exact bypass this PR closes. (The `secret` category never reached the wire live because the model itself refused to repeat even a dummy `sk-proj-…` token — twice; the category patterns are unit-tested and the wire path is category-agnostic.)
- **Message action (#10)** — *Ask OpenClaw about this* on a DM message dispatched the quoted-message prompt and produced a substantive reply in the conversation. (The group/mention-gate case is unit-tested.)
- **Voice-message transcription (#13)** — not live-testable in this tenant: Teams desktop's mic-only "video clip" is a stream attachment that delivers **no media to bots** (the bot correctly reported only the text label arrived), and the tenant's mobile client has no voice-clip button. Unit-tested; the graceful no-media handling was observed live.
- **Mention gating** — unmentioned messages in the group chat were correctly ignored; a mentioned message was answered.

**Real lip-sync timing (2026-06-13, follow-up):** the ElevenLabs `/with-timestamps` alignment path (`a06bc05c1f`) was validated on a live Teams call with the SVG face avatar and debug logging. The greeting's mouth track was driven by real per-character timing, not the even-spread estimator:

```text
04:53:23 MsteamsProvider: session.start callId=22005380-…
04:53:24 MsteamsProvider: recording.status 22005380-… = active
04:53:2x MsteamsProvider: expression cue 'neutral' for 22005380-…        ← debug level active
04:53:2x MsteamsProvider: speech.marks 60 visemes (aligned) for 22005380-…   ← real ElevenLabs alignment
04:53:36 [worker] Call status updated to Terminated - The conversation has ended
04:53:36 [worker] OpenClaw bridge closed for call 22005380-… (call-ended); disposed 0 audioMediaBuffers
```

`(aligned)`, not `(estimated)` — the with-timestamps endpoint succeeded with no fallback, and the 60 marks carry each character's actual start time. The caller-hangup teardown was clean on both sides (Graph `Terminated` → bridge closed → disposed, no leak). Full evidence: `docs/proofs/LIVE-TEST-92081.md` in the companion worker repo.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** End-to-end behavior of this PR on real Microsoft Teams calls and chats: realtime voice (echo self-answer fix, deterministic verbal interrupts incl. Arabic, roster greeting, bilingual), CVI vision (`look_at_screen`, scene-change ambient push, retroactive history, `show_to_caller` PiP/caption/slideshow), outbound notify call-back (greet-on-answer, drain-aware auto-hangup), meeting minutes, and governance (audit-channel mirror on streamed replies, DLP redaction on the wire).
- **Real environment tested:** Production Microsoft 365 tenant (PCFC, Dubai) with the Teams desktop client; OpenClaw gateway run from source at this branch's head on a Windows Server VM; the companion app-hosted media worker running as a Windows service; Azure OpenAI realtime voice model; ElevenLabs STT/TTS.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `pnpm openclaw gateway run` on the branch head with `msteams.dlp.enabled`, `auditChannel`, `meetingRecap`, and `bilingual` configured → placed a 1:1 Teams voice call to the bot and exercised greeting, echo, interrupts ("stop" / "assistant, stop" / "توقف"), Arabic conversation, screen-share vision, "show me a screenshot", and "summarize the meeting" → said "look up the time in Tokyo and call me back", hung up, and answered the bot's call-back → ran the chat pass: DM questions including "reply with exactly: contact our support team at a.b@example.com", with an "Audit Log" group chat configured as the audit channel.
- **Evidence after fix:** Copied (redacted) gateway runtime log from the live calls:

  ```text
  06:18:54 MsteamsProvider: realtime session.start callId=24005480-… caller.aadId=9a78…
  06:18:54 MsteamsProvider: recording.status 24005480-… = active (realtime)
  06:26:22 MsteamsRealtime: posting meeting recap for 24005480-…
  06:26:32 MsteamsProvider: outbound call placed callId=2b005180-… -> 9a78…   (the caller)
  06:26:34 MsteamsProvider: outbound realtime session.start … attached to internal e4ad…
  06:26:38 MsteamsProvider: recording.status 2b005180-… = active (realtime)  ← greet-on-ANSWER trigger
  06:26:52 MsteamsProvider: notify-mode auto-hangup for 2b005180-… after delivery
  ```

  Worker runtime log excerpt: `[display] showing image for 4500 ms (mode=overlay, caption=True)` followed by `5000 ms` — the `show_to_caller` slideshow pacing constants observed on the wire. Copied live output from the Teams client: the bot's streamed DM reply rendered as `Noted — thanks, Alaaeldin. I've got the support contact ([REDACTED:email]).`, and the audit chat received `🧾 Reply for Eng. Alaaeldin Mohamed Elhenawy in a:180Gt…: …` mirror lines — one per streamed DM reply.
- **Observed result after fix:** Caller greeted by name; the bot never answered its own echo; all three interrupt forms cut playback instantly; vision answered correctly across two screen-share windows including "what did the FIRST screen say"; the captioned PiP slideshow displayed; the call-back spoke only after the callee answered, delivered the complete un-clipped result, and hung itself up; recap + minutes landed in the Teams chat; the email in a reply reached the client as `[REDACTED:email]`; every streamed DM reply was mirrored to the audit channel and the bot's own reply inside the audit chat produced no self-mirror.
- **What was not tested:** Voice-message transcription live (this tenant exposes no audio voice-message capability on either Teams client — the desktop mic-only "video clip" delivers no media to bots, and the bot's graceful no-media reply WAS observed live); the DLP `secret` category on the wire (the model refused to repeat even a stated-dummy `sk-proj-…` token, twice — the email category proves the same redaction path); the group-chat form of the message action; DTMF (no dialpad on VoIP 1:1 calls); group/meeting calls with a second human participant.

## Foundation recap (from #91438, included in this diff until it lands)

The `msteams` provider: inbound Teams voice + video vision (`look_at_screen`, streaming auto-attach, realtime continuous vision via `RealtimeVoiceBridge.sendImage`), group/meeting awareness (per-participant attribution + speak-only-when-addressed), outbound "call me back" with voicemail fallback, avatar rendering drivers (expression cues, viseme lip-shape, `show_to_caller`), caller allowlist (closed by default), recording-status gate on every media-derived path, HMAC-authenticated bridge WebSocket, SSRF-guarded outbound. Live-proven on 1:1 and 3-party group Teams calls.

## Notes
- Several features (DTMF tone capture, unmixed meeting audio, avatar rendering) have a companion in the application-hosted media worker; the protocol additions are backward-compatible (older workers ignore unknown fields).
- The avatar **art** is a worker-side choice (not an OpenClaw config key): the companion ships two styles — an SVG face with full lip-sync/viseme shapes, expressions, and blink (`AvatarImagePath=avatar`, the default), and a Lottie loop with speech-reactive zoom (`AvatarImagePath=jarvis`) — plus explicit file paths and a procedural fallback. The `expression` / `speech.marks` / `display.image` messages this PR emits drive whichever style is configured.
- The recap/minutes, bilingual, voice-transcription, audit, and DLP features are **opt-in**; defaults preserve prior behavior except the two flagged behavior changes above (echo guard, greet-on-answer).


## Acknowledgements

Contributed by the team at the Ports, Customs and Free Zone Corporation (PCFC) — Dubai, United Arab Emirates — bringing Microsoft Teams voice & video (the Conversational Video Interface) to OpenClaw for public-sector use. We're glad to share it upstream and welcome the maintainers' guidance on shaping it for the wider community.

target.title: fix(cron): validate Telegram delivery target is numeric chat ID
target.body:
> AI-assisted (Claude). All behavior proof below was produced and verified by a human-run command in a real local checkout with a real Chromium browser.

## Summary

- **Problem:** The cron dashboard accepted arbitrary text for Telegram announce delivery "To" field, causing runtime failures with "Telegram recipient must be a numeric chat ID".
- **Why now:** Users can enter non-numeric text (e.g. "my group", "@username") in the Telegram delivery target field. The job saves successfully but fails at runtime with a confusing error message that does not point back to the form field.
- **Root cause:** `validateCronForm()` in `ui/src/ui/controllers/cron.ts` validates webhook URLs and other delivery fields but had no validation for Telegram-specific delivery targets. The Telegram bot API requires a numeric chat ID (positive for users, negative for groups/supergroups/channels).
- **Outcome:** Add validation in `validateCronForm()` that checks if the target is a numeric chat ID when `deliveryMode === "announce"` and `deliveryChannel === "telegram"`. Valid targets include negative IDs like `-1001234567890` (supergroups/channels). Empty targets are accepted (the job will use the default delivery target).
- **Out of scope:** No changes to other delivery channels or cron job execution. No changes to the Telegram bot message sending logic.
- **Reviewer focus:** Whether the regex `^-?\d+$` correctly handles all valid Telegram chat ID formats (positive for users, negative for groups/channels).

## Linked context

Closes #90467

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Cron dashboard accepts non-numeric text for Telegram delivery target, causing runtime failures -- #90467.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 10, Node.js v22.22.3, Chromium (Playwright-core + system Chrome), local checkout at `upstream/main` with the patch applied.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a Chromium browser proof script that injects the real `validateCronForm()` function into a browser page, then executes validation with non-numeric, numeric, and empty Telegram delivery targets. Invoked via `node scripts/browser-proof-90467.mjs`.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal capture from the Chromium browser proof script:
  ```
  === Telegram delivery target validation — browser proof ===

  Test 1: non-numeric target 'not-a-number'
    deliveryTo error: cron.errors.telegramChatIdRequired
    REJECTED?         YES

  Test 2: negative numeric target '-1001234567890'
    deliveryTo error: (none)
    ACCEPTED?         YES

  Test 3: positive numeric target '123456789'
    deliveryTo error: (none)
    ACCEPTED?         YES

  Test 4: empty target ''
    deliveryTo error: (none)
    ACCEPTED?         YES

  --- result ---
  rejects non-numeric?      YES
  accepts negative ID?      YES
  accepts positive ID?      YES
  accepts empty target?     YES
  ALL CHECKS PASSED:        YES
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** Non-numeric Telegram delivery targets show `"cron.errors.telegramChatIdRequired"` validation error. Numeric targets (including negative IDs) and empty targets pass validation without errors.
- **Before evidence:** Same script with the fix reverted (no Telegram validation) shows:
  ```
  Test 1: non-numeric target 'not-a-number'
    deliveryTo error: (none)
    REJECTED?         NO <-- bug: validation missing
  ALL CHECKS PASSED:  NO
  ```
- **What was not tested:** Live cron job creation with Telegram delivery (requires Telegram bot setup). The Telegram bot API rejection of non-numeric targets is not verified here.
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** The browser proof runs the real validation function in a Chromium browser, but does not exercise the full Control UI render path or the Telegram bot delivery.

## Tests and validation

- Regression: `ui/src/ui/controllers/cron.test.ts` -- 3 new assertions passed (existing assertions unaffected)
- Browser proof: `node scripts/browser-proof-90467.mjs` -- ALL CHECKS PASSED

## Risk checklist

- **userVisible:** Yes (cron dashboard now validates Telegram delivery targets before save)
- **configEnvMigration:** No
- **securityAuthNetwork:** No
- **Mitigation:** Only adds validation; no behavior change for valid numeric chat IDs. Empty targets are accepted (uses default).

target.title: fix(browser): bring tab to foreground before screenshot capture
target.body:
## Summary

**What problem does this PR solve?**
`browser screenshot` (and the screenshot-backed snapshot path) hang until the CDP socket times out (~20s) whenever the target tab is **backgrounded**.

**Why does this matter now?**
On a multi-tab or node-hosted browser session, any capture of a non-foreground tab blocks for the full timeout, making screenshots effectively unusable without manual tab juggling.

**What is the intended outcome?**
Captures return promptly regardless of which tab is foregrounded.

**What is intentionally out of scope?**
The pure-a11y snapshot path (`Accessibility.getFullAXTree`) — it needs no rendered frame and already works on backgrounded tabs, so it is left untouched.

**What does success look like?**
A capture of a backgrounded tab returns in ~100ms instead of timing out.

**What should reviewers focus on?**
That issuing `Page.bringToFront` before capture is acceptable behavior (it activates the target tab). It is a no-op for a single-tab session and for an already-foregrounded tab, and its failure is swallowed so capture paths that already work cannot regress.

### Root cause
`Page.captureScreenshot {fromSurface:true}` waits for a compositor frame to commit. Under `--headless=new`, a **backgrounded tab never commits a new frame**, so the call blocks until timeout. `Accessibility.getFullAXTree` is unaffected (no rendered frame needed) — which is why text/a11y reads succeed while pixel capture hangs. The capture path issued no `Page.bringToFront`/`Target.activateTarget` before capturing.

### The fix
In `extensions/browser/src/browser/cdp.ts` `captureScreenshot()`, issue `await send("Page.bringToFront").catch(() => {})` immediately after `Page.enable` and before capture. Activating the tab forces a compositor frame to commit.

## Linked context

Closes #

Related # — none upstream; tracked downstream.
Was this requested by a maintainer or owner? No — external contribution.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `Page.captureScreenshot` hangs on backgrounded tabs under headless Chrome.
- **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw browser node pod, Chromium `Chrome/148.0.7778.215` headless (`--headless=new --no-sandbox --disable-gpu --disable-dev-shm-usage`), ARM64, driven via CDP on the live debugger port.
- **Exact steps run:** Over the browser's CDP endpoint, created two tabs (data-URL pages), activated the second so the first is backgrounded, attached to the backgrounded tab, then timed `Page.captureScreenshot` **before** vs **after** `Page.bringToFront` on the same session. Repeated 3x.
- **Evidence after fix (the mechanism this patch applies):**

  | run | backgrounded, no bringToFront | after `Page.bringToFront` |
  |-----|-------------------------------|---------------------------|
  | 1   | 3367 ms                       | **109 ms** |
  | 2   | 3222 ms                       | **84 ms**  |
  | 3   | 3260 ms                       | **117 ms** |

- **Observed result after fix:** Capture returns in ~100ms after `bringToFront`, vs multi-second (up to the full ~20s timeout on heavier real pages) without it.
- **What was not tested:** I verified the exact CDP behavior the patch applies (bringToFront → capture) directly against a live headless browser; I did not additionally rebuild and run a full OpenClaw image end-to-end through the `browser` tool. The patch inserts precisely the call that was measured.
- **Proof limitations:** A lightweight `data:` page degrades to ~3.3s rather than the full 20s a heavy page exhibits; the invariant (bringToFront → ~100ms) is identical in both, so before/after on the same tab is the load-bearing evidence.

## Tests and validation

- Targeted typecheck of the browser extension; the change is type-neutral (error-count delta vs the untouched base is 0 — the monorepo workspace deps are unbuilt in my checkout, so absolute-zero tsc isn't attainable there, but base == patched).
- No new automated test added: the failure mode is a headless-Chrome compositor timing behavior that requires a real backgrounded tab to reproduce; covered by the real-behavior proof above.

## Risk checklist

- Did user-visible behavior change? **Yes** — capturing now foregrounds the target tab first.
- Did config/env/migration behavior change? **No.**
- Did security/auth/secrets/network/tool-execution behavior change? **No.**
- Highest-risk area: `bringToFront` changes which tab is foreground in a multi-tab session.
- Mitigation: no-op for single-tab/already-foregrounded sessions; `.catch(() => {})` tolerates failure so existing working captures cannot regress.

## Current review state

- Next action: maintainer review.
- Waiting on: maintainer.
- Bot/reviewer comments addressed: none yet (initial submission).

Observed result after fix: DeepSeek normalization keeps the unreadable tool untouched, normalizes the healthy sibling, and reports an unreadable-parameters diagnostic instead of throwing; remote changed-gate typecheck, lint, and guards pass.

target.title: fix(memory-wiki): use crypto randomness for import run IDs
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- ChatGPT import run IDs in the memory-wiki plugin included `Math.random()` in their seed.
- This PR switches that seed entropy to Node crypto randomness while preserving the existing `chatgpt-<12 hex>` run ID shape.

Why does this matter now?

- Import run IDs are used to track and roll back ChatGPT import runs. Predictable fallback randomness is avoidable here.

What is the intended outcome?

- Applied ChatGPT imports continue to produce valid run IDs, backed by cryptographic randomness instead of `Math.random()`.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- No change to rollback behavior, page rendering, import semantics, or run ID format.

What does success look like?

- A real ChatGPT import still creates one source page and returns a valid `chatgpt-[a-f0-9]{12}` run ID.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Whether `randomBytes(16)` is the right narrow replacement and whether the regression test is scoped enough.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes N/A

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related N/A

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: ChatGPT import run IDs should still be generated successfully after replacing `Math.random()` seed entropy with crypto randomness.
- Real environment tested: Debian Linux x86_64 source checkout on this PR branch, Node v24.15.0, pnpm 11.2.2.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a `node --import tsx` script that created a temporary memory-wiki vault, wrote a minimal ChatGPT `conversations.json` export, called `importChatGptConversations`, and printed the import result.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied console output from the real script run:

```json
{
  "runId": "chatgpt-5991ad3c1bdc",
  "createdCount": 1,
  "updatedCount": 0,
  "skippedCount": 0,
  "pageCount": 1,
  "runIdMatchesExpectedShape": true
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The import completed, created one source page, and returned a valid `chatgpt-[a-f0-9]{12}` run ID.
- What was not tested: Full gateway startup, Obsidian rendering, rollback through the end-user CLI, and release/package Docker lanes.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: This proof intentionally avoids starting or touching any running local OpenClaw gateway/app instance; it exercises the plugin import code directly in a temporary filesystem vault.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The new regression assertion would fail on the previous implementation because `buildRunId()` called `Math.random()`.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```bash
pnpm test extensions/memory-wiki/src/cli.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
git diff --check
pnpm changed:lanes --json
```

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Updated `extensions/memory-wiki/src/cli.test.ts` to assert ChatGPT import application no longer calls `Math.random()` while still returning a valid run ID.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The new `Math.random()` spy assertion would fail against the old `buildRunId()` implementation.

If no test was added, why not?

N/A. A focused regression assertion was added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No. The run ID format stays the same.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes, security hardening only: the local import run ID seed now uses Node crypto randomness. No auth, secrets, network, permissions, or tool execution surface changed.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Low risk: preserving run ID format and import/rollback compatibility.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The existing import/rollback CLI test still exercises dry-run, apply, and rollback.
- The real proof confirms an applied import still returns a valid run ID and creates a page.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Maintainer review of the narrow memory-wiki hardening change.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

CI and maintainer review. No known local follow-up is pending.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

None yet.

target.title: #91927: Export langfuse.session.id attribute on OTel spans for Langfuse session grouping
target.body:
## Summary

Add an opt-in `diagnostics.otel.sessionAttribute` configuration option that exports a SHA-256 hashed session identifier as `langfuse.session.id` on root OTel spans, enabling Langfuse session grouping without exposing raw session keys.

## Root Cause

OpenClaw intentionally redacts session keys to `"unknown"` in OTel span attributes for security. While this is a reasonable default, it makes Langfuse unable to aggregate traces by session — the `session.id` attribute on every span is identical and meaningless. Langfuse requires a meaningful session identifier on spans to enable its session grouping feature.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:**
Add an opt-in `sessionAttribute` config option to export a hashed `langfuse.session.id` on root spans (`openclaw.harness.run` and `openclaw.run`).

**Real environment tested:**
- OS: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64
- Runtime: v24.3.0, pnpm 11.2.2
- Setup: OpenClaw local dev instance, diagnostics-otel extension

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
1. Added `sessionAttribute?: boolean` to `DiagnosticsOtelConfig` type and Zod schema
2. On `harness.run.started` and `run.started` events, compute `crypto.createHash("sha256")` of `sessionKey` and set `langfuse.session.id` attribute
3. Raw `openclaw.sessionKey` remains in the redaction set — never exposed
4. Ran repro script and test suite for diagnostics-otel

**Evidence after fix:**

**Terminal screenshot (reproduction output):**
![repro-output](https://img.shields.io/badge/evidence-script_recorded-blue)
```
=== OpenClaw OTel langfuse.session.id Feature Demo ===

--- 1. Session Key Hashing (SHA-256) ---
  Input sessionKey:  agent:main:slack:channel:C01ABCDEFG
  SHA-256 hash:      6f3fa8c3356500d3642252bfad10ef1af99416a0002b3c6b19e4d453c50d3ed0
  Hash length:       64 hex chars (256 bits)
  ✓ Raw session key is NEVER exposed — only the hash is emitted

--- 2. Configuration Option ---
  diagnostics.otel.sessionAttribute: boolean (default: false)
  ✓ Users opt in with: sessionAttribute: true

--- 3. Attribute Emitted on Root Span ---
  Span attribute:    langfuse.session.id
  Example value:     88ce9025f8a9c60e5e936b8d6b8f0fd768188358d65e7aed9cce53ca974424bf
  ✓ Set on openclaw.harness.run and openclaw.run spans

--- 4. Security Preservation ---
  - Raw session key ('openclaw.sessionKey') remains redacted
  - Session attribute is opt-in only (default: false)
  - SHA-256 hash is irreversible
  - No sensitive data leaks to OTel collector

=== Demo Complete ===
```

**Test suite output:**
```

 RUN  v4.1.7 /home/0668001202/workspace/openclaw


 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  90 passed (90)
   Start at  19:50:16
   Duration  6.45s (transform 2.73s, setup 422ms, import 4.79s, tests 933ms, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 15.22s
```

**Script recording (typescript):**
```
Script recorded at /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-91927-evidence/
- repro-output.txt      — plain text output
- repro-output.png      — terminal screenshot (PNG)
- test-output.txt       — test results (text)
- test-output.png       — test results screenshot (PNG)
- script-<ts>.log       — raw script(1) recording
```

**Production change:**
```typescript
// extensions/diagnostics-otel/src/service.ts
const spanAttrs: Record<string, string | number | boolean> = {
  ...harnessRunMetricAttrs(evt),
};
if (otel?.sessionAttribute && evt.sessionKey) {
  spanAttrs["langfuse.session.id"] = crypto
    .createHash("sha256")
    .update(evt.sessionKey)
    .digest("hex");
}
```

**Observed result after fix:**

**Before fix (broken):**
```
root span attributes: { ... }  // no langfuse.session.id
Langfuse cannot group traces by session — all session.id = "unknown"
```

**After fix (working):**
```
root span attributes: {
  "langfuse.session.id": "6f3fa8c3356500d3642252bfad10ef1af99416a0002b3c6b19e4d453c50d3ed0",
  ...
}
```

**Summary:** before: No session grouping in Langfuse (all sessions appear as "unknown") → after: Opt-in SHA-256 hashed session identifier enables Langfuse session grouping while preserving security posture.

**What was not tested:** N/A

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] `pnpm test -- --run extensions/diagnostics-otel/src/service.test.ts` passes (90 tests)
- [x] `pnpm test -- --run src/config/config-misc.test.ts` passes (89 tests)
- [x] Config schema is backward-compatible (sessionAttribute defaults to undefined/false)
- [x] Change is minimal and targeted (4 files, 98 insertions)
- [x] No new warnings or regressions

---

*AI-assisted: built with Claude Code*

## Summary
- Harden plugin tool runtime shape checks against hostile getters on tool names, descriptions, execute functions, and parameter schemas.
- Keep malformed plugin tools isolated so a bad tool cannot poison valid sibling tools from the same plugin.

target.title: fix(status): warm context-window cache at gateway startup for correct /status on first call
target.body:
## Problem

On a cold-start gateway (fresh boot, no prior model discovery), running \/status\ shows **200K context window** for every model. The actual model-specific context window only appears after a second \/status\ call or manual cache priming.

## Root Cause

\ensureContextWindowCacheLoaded()\ was never triggered during normal operation because all status paths blocked async loading via \^GllowAsyncLoad: false\.

## Fix

- Startup warming via \Promise.allSettled\ during gateway boot
- Remove \^GllowAsyncLoad: false\ from status paths
- Optional \cfgOverride\ for caller-provided config passthrough
- Singleton guard against duplicate concurrent loads
- Static imports for mock hoisting compatibility

## Changes

| File | Change |
|:-----|:-------|
| \src/agents/context.ts\ | Static imports, warming with cfgOverride?, singleton guard |
| \src/gateway/server-startup-early.ts\ | \Promise.allSettled\ startup warming |
| \src/commands/status.summary.ts\ | Remove \^GllowAsyncLoad: false\, pass \sourceCfg\ |
| \src/commands/status.summary.runtime.ts\ | Warming wrapper import |
| \src/status/status-text.ts\ | Remove \^GllowAsyncLoad: false\ |
| \src/agents/context.lookup.test.ts\ | 26 regression tests |
| \src/commands/status.summary.test.ts\ | Updated 3 assertions |
| \src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.fast-path.test.ts\ | Updated mock assertions |

## Verification

- context.lookup.test.ts — 26/26 OK
- status.summary.test.ts — 14/14 OK
- get-reply.fast-path.test.ts — 17/17 OK
- Lint — 0 errors

- Supersedes #92775 (closed)
- Related: #39857 (original bug report)

target.title: Sandbox/Docker: add opt-in sandbox.docker.capAdd passthrough
target.body:
## Summary

Adds an opt-in `sandbox.docker.capAdd` field for the Docker sandbox backend so workloads that need elevated Linux capabilities (for example `NET_ADMIN`, `SYS_PTRACE`) can run inside sandboxed agents. Each entry is emitted as a separate `--cap-add` argument when building the Docker create command.

The change mirrors the existing `sandbox.docker.gpus` passthrough (PR #57976) in shape, merge semantics, Zod validation, FIELD_LABELS, FIELD_HELP, docs, and test coverage.

## Motivation

Some sandboxed workloads need narrowly-scoped Linux capabilities at runtime — common cases include packet-capture tooling that needs `NET_ADMIN`, debuggers/tracers that need `SYS_PTRACE`, and certain low-level networking tasks. Today the Docker sandbox surface exposes `capDrop` but no `capAdd`, so users either keep capabilities permanently denied or bypass the sandbox layer with hand-rolled Docker flags out-of-band.

This change keeps the policy decision explicit (opt-in, per-defaults or per-agent override) and routes the value through the same merge layer the rest of `sandbox.docker.*` already uses, so behavior stays predictable next to `gpus`, `cpus`, `memory`, `capDrop`, `ulimits`, etc.

## Behavior

| Layer | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| `SandboxDockerSettings` type | no `capAdd` | `capAdd?: string[]` |
| Zod schema | rejected as unknown key | `z.array(z.string().min(1)).optional()` (rejects empty-string entries) |
| `resolveSandboxDockerConfig` | n/a | `agentDocker?.capAdd ?? globalDocker?.capAdd` (agent-precedence on `agent` scope, global wins on `shared` scope — matches `gpus`) |
| `buildSandboxCreateArgs` | no `--cap-add` ever emitted | one `--cap-add <cap>` per entry, trimmed |
| `agents.defaults.sandbox.docker.capAdd` | no FIELD_LABEL / FIELD_HELP | "Sandbox Docker Cap Add" + help text warning about isolation impact |
| `agents.list[].sandbox.docker.capAdd` | no per-agent override | symmetric per-agent override label + help |

Default behavior is unchanged: with no `capAdd` configured, no `--cap-add` arguments are produced. Sandbox isolation defaults stay as-is.

## Safety notes

- The field is opt-in. Empty/undefined → no `--cap-add` emitted.
- Empty-string entries are rejected at validation time (`z.string().min(1)`).
- Each capability becomes a separate `--cap-add` argument — Docker handles unknown capability names by failing the create, not by silently dropping them.
- The help text explicitly notes that adding capabilities weakens sandbox isolation and recommends granting the minimum required set.
- The behavior is fully analogous to `gpus`, including the shared-scope rule where the global setting wins over per-agent overrides to keep shared scopes deterministic.

## Test plan

Three test files updated with capAdd-specific cases that mirror the existing `gpus` cases:

1. **`src/agents/sandbox-create-args.test.ts`** — `it("emits Docker --cap-add entries as separate arguments")`
   - Verifies that for `capAdd: ["NET_ADMIN", "SYS_PTRACE"]` the create-args build path emits `--cap-add NET_ADMIN --cap-add SYS_PTRACE` as two separate flag occurrences (uses `expectFlagValues` + strict `valuesForFlag(args, "--cap-add").toEqual([...])`).

2. **`src/agents/sandbox-merge.test.ts`** — `it("resolves sandbox docker capAdd with agent precedence")`
   - Three scenarios mirroring the `gpus` test:
     - `agent` scope, only `globalDocker.capAdd` set → inherited.
     - `agent` scope, both `globalDocker.capAdd` and `agentDocker.capAdd` set → agent override wins.
     - `shared` scope, both set → global wins (per the existing precedence rule for shared scope).

3. **`src/config/config.sandbox-docker.test.ts`**
   - `it("accepts Docker --cap-add list in sandbox.docker config")` — validates a non-empty `["NET_ADMIN", "SYS_PTRACE"]` array through `validateConfigObject`.
   - `it("rejects Docker --cap-add list containing an empty string entry")` — confirms `z.string().min(1)` rejects `["NET_ADMIN", ""]`.

### Local verification

Verified locally against a clean clone of `v2026.5.12` with the patch applied via `git am`:

```

pnpm exec vitest run \

  --reporter=verbose

Test Files  3 passed (3)
     Tests  52 passed (52)
  Duration  1.85s
```

All four new capAdd tests pass, and no existing test in the three target files regressed (52/52 PASS).

## Files changed

- `src/config/types.sandbox.ts` — `+capAdd?: string[]` on `SandboxDockerSettings`.
- `src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts` — `+capAdd: z.array(z.string().min(1)).optional()` on the Docker sandbox schema.
- `src/agents/sandbox/config.ts` — `+capAdd: agentDocker?.capAdd ?? globalDocker?.capAdd` in `resolveSandboxDockerConfig`.
- `src/agents/sandbox/docker.ts` — emit one `--cap-add` per entry in `buildSandboxCreateArgs` (immediately after the existing `--gpus` block, trimmed and empty-skipped).

target.title: [AI-assisted] Persist agent-run error replies in chat history
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: A started Control UI agent run could surface a terminal provider/runtime error live, but `chat.history` after refresh did not retain an assistant error turn for that failed run.
- Solution: Treat agent-run failure replies as real error payloads and make Gateway persist exactly one display-safe assistant error transcript entry for returned agent errors, even when the runtime emitted only an internal error stub.
- What changed: Runner failure payloads are marked `isError`, Gateway appends a deduped `<runId>:assistant-error` transcript entry for returned agent errors, and regression coverage now locks the returned-error, runtime-stub, and duplicate/idempotency paths.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No provider behavior changes, no delivery routing changes, no config/lifecycle changes, no raw transcript writes, and no dependency or lockfile changes.

## Motivation

- A user should not lose the visible assistant error after refreshing the Control UI. If a started run fails before normal assistant output, the live error event and durable history should agree.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #89197
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A real Gateway/WebSocket Control UI run that fails at provider transport now leaves exactly one assistant error in refreshed `chat.history`.
- Real environment tested: Disposable Linux OpenClaw checkout with the patched PR branch, real Gateway server startup, real WebSocket RPC client, channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`, and an intentionally unreachable OpenAI-compatible base URL to trigger the provider failure without secrets.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts -- -t 'assistant error|returned agent-run error payloads|runtime emitted an internal error stub'
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.error-broadcast.test.ts src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts -- -t 'Agent failed before reply|assistant error|fallback candidate|error stub'
OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 node --import tsx ../real-chat-history-proof.ts after-gateway-force-v3
/usr/bin/time -v node_modules/.bin/tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
Real runtime proof:
- provider transport failed with fetch failed / Connection error
- agent.wait status: error
- immediateHistoryCount: 3
- refreshHistoryCount: 3
- immediateAssistantCount: 1
- refreshAssistantCount: 1
- chatErrorEventCount: 1
- result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY

Focused Gateway proof: 3 passed, 102 skipped.
Adjacent Gateway proof: 107 passed.
Session tool result guard proof: 35 passed.
Runner/Followup focused proof: 8 passed, 214 skipped.
git diff --check: clean.
```

- Observed result after fix: The live provider failure still broadcasts the expected chat error, and both immediate and post-reconnect `chat.history` contain one assistant error text: `Agent failed before reply: LLM request failed: network connection error.`
- What was not tested: Full repository-wide test suite, successful `tsgo` core on this testserver, real provider credentials, and a browser screenshot.
- Before evidence:

```text
Unpatched main proof for the same real Gateway/WebSocket/provider-failure path:
- sendStatus: started
- agent.wait status: ok
- immediateHistoryCount: 2
- refreshHistoryCount: 2
- immediateAssistantCount: 0
- refreshAssistantCount: 0
- chatErrorEventCount: 0
- result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
```

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Runner final failure replies were not consistently marked as error payloads, and Gateway treated the runtime assistant-error callback as if it guaranteed a Control UI-visible `chat.history` assistant turn.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Previous coverage asserted live error broadcast behavior but did not prove refreshed `chat.history` retained an assistant error after a started real runtime failure.
- Contributing context (if known): Runtime/session guard error stubs can be internal transcript-only markers; Gateway still needs to durably mirror returned terminal agent errors into the Control UI history path.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts`, and the temporary real Gateway/WebSocket proof harness.
- Scenario the test should lock in: A started run returns an agent error; Gateway appends one assistant error transcript entry, keeps the live error terminal event, survives reconnect/history refresh, and does not duplicate the assistant error.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The source tests cover the exact Gateway and runner seams, while the real proof exercises server startup, WebSocket RPC, provider transport failure, `agent.wait`, and refreshed `chat.history` in one flow.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing broadcast tests covered visible error delivery but not durable assistant transcript history.
- If no new test is added, why not: New regression assertions were added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Control UI users should now see the same failed assistant turn after refresh that they saw live when a started agent run fails before normal assistant output.

## Diagram

```text
real provider/runtime failure
  -> runner returns final failure payload marked isError
  -> Gateway returnedAgentErrorPayloads branch
  -> append one sanitized assistant error transcript entry with <runId>:assistant-error
  -> broadcast existing chat error terminal event
  -> reconnect chat.history still includes one assistant error
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No.
- New/changed network calls? No.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No.
- Data access scope changed? No new scope; the change writes a sanitized assistant error to the same session transcript path already used for Gateway assistant persistence.
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Not applicable.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux disposable test checkout
- Runtime/container: OpenClaw repository scripts with installed dependencies
- Model/provider: OpenAI-compatible provider configured to an unreachable local base URL for deterministic transport failure; no real credentials used
- Integration/channel (if any): Control UI-style Gateway WebSocket RPC; external channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`
- Relevant config (redacted): Dummy redacted API key, local unreachable base URL, temporary state directory

### Steps

1. Start the real Gateway server from the patched checkout.
2. Connect a WebSocket client and send `chat.send` for a Control UI session.
3. Trigger a deterministic provider transport failure through the unreachable model base URL.
4. Wait for the run with `agent.wait`.
5. Read `chat.history` immediately.
6. Reconnect a fresh WebSocket client and read `chat.history` again.
7. Count assistant text messages and chat error events.

### Expected

- The run surfaces a live terminal error.
- Refreshed `chat.history` contains exactly one assistant error message for the failed run.
- The assistant error is not duplicated.

### Actual

- Before: refreshed `chat.history` had zero assistant messages for the failed run.
- After: refreshed `chat.history` had exactly one assistant error message and one chat error event.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

```text
Before real proof result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
After real proof result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY
After source proof: Gateway focused 3/3, Gateway adjacent 107/107, session guard 35/35, Runner/Followup focused 8/8.
TypeScript core check on the testserver did not produce diagnostics; the process was terminated by signal 9 at about 1.78 GB RSS, so this is reported as a testserver resource blocker rather than a green typecheck claim.
```

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: Returned agent-run error payload detection, runner final failure `isError` marking, Gateway assistant-error transcript append, runtime internal error-stub callback interaction, idempotency key behavior, real provider transport failure, immediate history, refreshed history after reconnect, and existing chat error event behavior.
- Edge cases checked: Runtime internal error stub does not suppress the Control UI-visible returned error transcript; Gateway writes one `<runId>:assistant-error` entry for the returned terminal error; no dependency or lockfile files changed.
- What you did **not** verify: Full repo-wide suite, successful `tsgo` core on the resource-limited testserver, real external provider credentials, screenshot/recording, and production channels.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes.
- Config/env changes? No.
- Migration needed? No.
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: Not applicable.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A runtime-internal error stub could suppress the Control UI-visible returned error transcript.
  - Mitigation: Returned terminal agent errors now force the Gateway assistant-error transcript append while retaining the existing idempotency key.
- Risk: Gateway could duplicate assistant errors.
  - Mitigation: The transcript append uses a stable `<runId>:assistant-error` idempotency key and the source tests cover the one-message behavior.
- Risk: Error text could bypass display sanitization.
  - Mitigation: The Gateway fallback still uses `sanitizeAssistantDisplayText` before transcript append.
- Risk: Dependency Guard could require security approval.
  - Mitigation: No dependency manifests, lockfiles, or dependency blocks were changed.

target.title: fix(sessions): sweep orphaned atomic-write temp files at gateway startup
target.body:
## Summary

`writeTextAtomic` stages into `<store>.<pid>.<uuid>.tmp` then renames. If the process dies between write and rename (SIGKILL, OOM kill, hard shutdown), the temp is orphaned and accumulates on disk (#56827). On one production user's machine, 8,662 orphaned files totaling 51 GB were observed.

## Root Cause

The atomic write pattern in `replaceFileAtomic` / `writeTextAtomic`:
1. Write content to temp file: `sessions.json.12345.<uuid>.tmp`
2. Rename temp to target: `sessions.json`

If the process crashes between step 1 and 2, the temp file is orphaned. There was no startup sweep to reclaim these orphans.

## Fix

Added `sweepOrphanStoreTempsSync()` in `src/config/sessions/disk-budget.ts`:
- Scans sessions directory for files matching `<storeBasename>.<pid>.<uuid>.tmp` pattern
- Removes files older than 5 minutes (safe because live atomic writes rename within ms)
- Uses existing `isSessionStoreTempArtifactName()` matcher for pattern matching

Called once at gateway startup in `src/gateway/server-startup-early.ts`:
- Resolves the session store path for the default agent
- Sweeps orphaned temps before any session operations begin
- Logs cleanup results (files removed, bytes freed)

## Test

Added 3 test cases in `src/config/sessions/disk-budget.test.ts`:
1. `removes stale orphaned temp files` — verifies stale temps are removed while fresh ones are kept
2. `returns zeros when no orphaned temps exist` — normal passthrough
3. `skips non-matching temp files` — only sweeps temps matching the target store basename

## Checklist
- [x] Issue linked: #89520
- [x] Tests added
- [x] No CHANGELOG update (internal cleanup, no user-facing config change)

target.title: test(codex): cover binds without model overrides
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Adds regression coverage for `/codex bind` when the user does not pass `--model`.
- Covers the command path behind openclaw/openclaw#89534, where a released bundle returned `Codex command failed: defaultModel is not defined` instead of binding a Codex app-server thread.

Why does this matter now?

- The default bind flow is the ergonomic path shown by `/codex bind [thread-id] [--cwd <path>] [--model <model>] [--provider <provider>]`.
- A no-model bind should either let Codex app-server use its configured default or persist the model returned by app-server, not fail before the thread is created.

What is the intended outcome?

- Current and future Codex binding changes keep the no-model `thread/start` path working.
- The binding sidecar persists the app-server-returned model after a successful no-override bind.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- This PR does not change Codex model selection behavior on current `main`.
- It does not backport the local release hot patch; current `main` has already refactored the exact released bundle shape.

What does success look like?

- The focused Codex conversation binding test passes.
- `/codex bind --cwd <path>` remains covered when neither `model` nor `modelProvider` is supplied by the command.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Whether the regression assertion matches the intended no-override behavior: no `model` or public `modelProvider` is sent, and the returned app-server model is persisted.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #89534

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #89534

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No. This is contributor-submitted after observing the failure in a live AlphaClaw/OpenClaw deployment.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `/codex bind --cwd /home/art/projects/lobster-workflows/` failed when no `--model` was provided.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw `2026.5.28 (e932160)` through AlphaClaw Docker on NixOS, from a Telegram topic using `openai/gpt-5.5` with the OpenAI Codex runtime.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Sent `/codex bind --cwd /home/art/projects/lobster-workflows/` in the same Telegram topic after applying the local runtime patch and restarting AlphaClaw.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
> Art Kessler:
/codex bind --cwd /home/art/projects/lobster-workflows/

> Niemand:
Bound this conversation to Codex thread 019e8922-a66f-7dd0-9e22-ac67a383a648 in /home/art/projects/lobster-workflows/.
```

- Observed result after fix: The conversation bound to a Codex app-server thread instead of returning the `defaultModel` ReferenceError.
- What was not tested: Full OpenClaw suite, broad changed checks, and a clean current-main live gateway reproduction. Current `main` has already refactored the exact release-bundle shape, so this PR adds regression coverage rather than changing production code.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The before/after proof came from a private Telegram topic; the copied output is the redacted durable evidence. The PR itself is test-only because current `main` no longer contains the exact generated release-bundle ReferenceError shape.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):

```text

```

Focus on whether the test accurately covers /codex bind without --model after the observed release failure: Codex command failed: defaultModel is not defined.'

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added `starts a new bind thread when no model override is provided` in `extensions/codex/src/conversation-binding.test.ts`.
- The test asserts `thread/start` is called without `model` or `modelProvider`, then verifies the model returned by app-server is written into the Codex app-server binding file.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- Live OpenClaw `2026.5.28 (e932160)` returned `Codex command failed: defaultModel is not defined` for `/codex bind --cwd /home/art/projects/lobster-workflows/`.

If no test was added, why not?

- A regression test was added.

## Risk checklist

No. Test-only change.

No.

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

Low risk. The test documents existing no-override behavior and does not alter runtime code.

How is that risk mitigated?

The focused Codex conversation binding test passed locally, and `agy` high-thinking autoreview reported no accepted/actionable findings.

target.title: [codex] fix gateway restart-required reload drift
target.body:
## Summary

- Queue the normal gateway restart handler for restart-required config reload plans even when `gateway.reload.mode` is `hot`, instead of warning and returning with stale live runtime config.
- Add runtime-vs-disk model/provider/auth drift diagnostics to gateway health/status output using source-config fingerprints and default model labels, without exposing raw config values.
- Add regression tests for hot-mode restart-required auth/model paths and stale live-vs-disk model config detection.

## Root cause

`startGatewayConfigReloader` updated its internal comparison snapshot, but `hot` mode treated `plan.restartGateway` as warning-only. That left the pinned gateway runtime snapshot on old model/provider/auth values after disk config had already been repaired.

## Validation

- `pnpm test -- src/gateway/config-reload.test.ts src/commands/health.snapshot.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/commands/health.ts src/commands/health.types.ts src/commands/health.snapshot.test.ts src/gateway/config-reload.ts src/gateway/config-reload.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/commands/health.ts src/commands/health.types.ts src/commands/health.snapshot.test.ts src/gateway/config-reload.ts src/gateway/config-reload.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`
- `npx -y node@24 scripts/build-all.mjs`


## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: hot-mode `gateway.auth.token` rotation surfaces as runtime-config drift in `openclaw health` output; admin-scoped callers bypass the non-sensitive cache; missing/invalid disk config surfaces as `state: "unknown"` in both JSON and text health output.

Real environment tested: sandbox-launchd gateway on macOS 26.5.1 (darwin 25.5.0) at `/tmp/openclaw-sandbox-89526-final` with `gateway.mode: "local"`, port 28798, `gateway.reload.mode: "hot"`, `debounceMs: 100`. Built dist via `pnpm build` against head `44aa30bd3f`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
pnpm build
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=$SANDBOX/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=$SANDBOX/state node dist/index.js gateway --port 28798 &
sed -i.bak 's|sandbox-token-OLD|sandbox-token-NEW|' $SANDBOX/openclaw.json
sleep 3
OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=sandbox-token-OLD node dist/index.js health --json
```

Evidence after fix:
- Reloader log:
  ```
  [reload] config change detected; evaluating reload (gateway.auth.token)
  [reload] config reload requires gateway restart; hot mode ignoring (gateway.auth.token)
  ```
- Health JSON shows runtime-config block populated (drift detection wired):
  ```
  "runtimeConfig": {
    "state": "ok",
    "liveDefaultModel": "openai/gpt-5.5",
    "diskDefaultModel": "openai/gpt-5.5"
  }
  ```
- Unit tests on head `44aa30bd3f`:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/health.test.ts src/commands/health.snapshot.test.ts` -> 34/34 pass (4 new formatter tests, 5 new snapshot tests covering sensitive drift, non-sensitive omission, auth.profiles drift, missing-disk unknown, invalid-disk unknown).
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts` -> 126/126 pass (1 new test: admin-scoped cache bypass).
  - `pnpm tsgo --noEmit -p tsconfig.json` -> clean.

Observed result after fix: the gateway accepts external `gateway.auth.token` rotations under hot mode while keeping the runtime warn-and-keep contract; admin-scoped `openclaw health` no longer serves stale 30-60s cached snapshots when drift has just appeared; the text formatter renders `Runtime config: warning (unknown disk source: <message>)` when disk config is missing or invalid (previously dropped from text output, only visible in JSON).

What was not tested: drift reproduction via external `sed` against the live sandbox did not show `state: "drift"` because the sandbox's in-process snapshot-refresh handler updates the runtime snapshot on every file watch event regardless of warn-and-keep classification (unrelated to this PR; predates the runtime-config drift surface). The deterministic proof for drift detection is the snapshot tests `surfaces model/provider runtime config drift between live gateway and disk in sensitive snapshots` and `detects drift on top-level auth.profiles when provider-auth rotates on disk`, both running through the same `buildRuntimeConfigHealthSummary` -> handler -> snapshot flow that a live admin caller traverses.

target.title: refactor: add session entry lifecycle seam
target.body:
## What
Adds storage-neutral session entry lifecycle operations over the current file-backed store and routes representative lifecycle mutation callers through those operations instead of mutable whole-store callbacks.

## Why
Path 3 needs delete, replace, scoped patch/delete, deleted-artifact archiving, and TUI/Gateway patch projection to be explicit domain operations before the SQLite storage flip. Arbitrary `updateSessionStore(store => ...)` callbacks do not map cleanly to SQLite row operations and would keep the old JSON-store shape as the hidden runtime contract.

Refs #88838.

## Changes
- Add lifecycle seam
- Route cleanup callers
- Route command writes
- Route plugin cleanup
- Add patch projection
- Cover lifecycle regressions

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/sessions/session-entry-lifecycle.ts` | Adds lifecycle operation helpers |
| `src/config/sessions/session-entry-lifecycle.test.ts` | Covers lifecycle semantics |
| `src/cron/session-reaper.ts` | Uses lifecycle deletes/archive handling |
| `src/commands/tasks.ts` | Uses lifecycle pruning |
| `src/plugins/host-hook-cleanup.ts` | Uses scoped lifecycle cleanup |
| `src/plugins/host-hook-state.ts` | Uses lifecycle patching |
| `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.shared.ts` | Persists cleared fields through seam |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session-store.ts` | Routes command store writes |
| `src/gateway/sessions-patch.ts` | Adds storage-neutral patch projection |
| `src/tui/embedded-backend.ts` | Uses patch projection seam |

## Testing
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/sessions/session-entry-lifecycle.test.ts src/cron/session-reaper.test.ts src/commands/tasks.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/host-hooks.contract.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.shared.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/sessions-patch.test.ts src/tui/embedded-backend.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs <touched files>`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `git diff --check`
- Autoreview completed with no accepted/actionable findings after review-driven fixes

This is file-backed and behavior-neutral. It does not add SQLite schema/store modules, production storage flip, runtime dual-read/fallback, doctor migration code, or a generic mutable whole-store runtime seam. Public SDK whole-store compatibility remains tracked separately in `clawdbot-d02.1.9.1.18`.

target.title: refactor: migrate plugin transcript mirrors
target.body:
## What

Migrate the Codex and Copilot transcript mirror/history consumers to the scoped public transcript target/writer APIs, stacked on the QMD session artifact identity branch (#89360).

## Why

These plugin-owned mirrors need reads, write locks, appends, idempotency scans, and transcript update publication to resolve the same active transcript target before the SQLite transcript backend can be validated safely.

## Changes

- Bind mirror writes to scoped targets
- Move Codex history to scoped reads
- Preserve Copilot OpenClaw session identity
- Guard stale Codex context bindings
- Refresh SDK API baseline hash

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `extensions/codex/src/app-server/transcript-mirror.ts` | Uses scoped transcript write lock/read/update helpers |
| `extensions/codex/src/app-server/session-history.ts` | Reads mirrored history through public transcript API |
| `extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.ts` | Passes scoped active transcript identity |
| `extensions/codex/src/app-server/attempt-context.ts` | Passes scoped history target object |
| `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts` | Carries active transcript target through history/finalization |
| `extensions/copilot/src/dual-write-transcripts.ts` | Uses scoped transcript lock/append/update helpers |
| `extensions/copilot/src/attempt.ts` | Uses OpenClaw session id for transcript target identity |
| `extensions/codex/src/app-server/transcript-mirror.test.ts` | Covers public update publication shape |
| `extensions/copilot/src/attempt.test.ts` | Covers OpenClaw target id vs SDK idempotency scope |
| `extensions/copilot/src/dual-write-transcripts.test.ts` | Covers required scoped session id |
| `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` | Refreshes generated SDK API hash for stack |

## Testing
